messages.content: paraphrase Social Efficiency educators believe curriculum objectives must be stated in behavioural terms: as observable skills, as capabilities for action, as activities people can perform, as demonstrable things people can do. The need to state objectives this way provides several insights into the Social Efficiency ideology. First, the behavioural phrasing of objectives reflects the Social Efficiency conception of the nature of man: the essence of man is expressed in the specific behaviors he can perform. Important here are both the behavioural conception of man and the conception of man as a bundle of specific skills. As Bobbitt wrote, “human life, however varied, consists in the performance of specific activities.” Second, the behavioural phrasing of objectives reflects the Social Efficiency conception of the nature of education.

In Social Efficiency education, it is believed that curriculum objectives should be articulated in terms of observable actions, skills, and tangible outcomes. This approach offers several insights into the ideology of Social Efficiency. Firstly, the way in which objectives are phrased reflects the belief that human essence is manifested through specific behaviors. This includes both the conceptualization of individuals as beings defined by their actions and the emphasis placed on possessing specific skills. Bobbitt, for example, argued that "specific activities" constitute the essence of human life. Secondly, the emphasis on behavioral phrasing reflects the Social Efficiency view of education, which emphasizes the acquisition of practical and applicable skills that enable individuals to succeed in the real world.

paraphrase HCT has become the argument of choice for the ‘Left modernisers’ over the ‘New Right’, who were motivated by a crass understanding of the market based entirely on notions of ‘choice’ and low cost, whilst the former seeks a more sustainable, long-term economy in which more people can have ‘full employability’ if not ‘full employment’. The key for the Left modernisers is the development of ‘human capital’ and the creation of a ‘knowledge economy’ (Benade, 2009). These thinkers are ‘tech savvy’ and see the rapid growth and development of technological solutions as central to their vision of a mobile, wired, modern global economy in which it behoves even a tiny player like New Zealand to ‘get up to speed’ to make a contribution on the global capitalist stage

The concept of human capital theory (HCT) has gained favor among "Left modernizers" as opposed to the "New Right," who are solely focused on the market and its low-cost options. Instead, the Left modernizers look to create a sustainable economy that allows for more people to have the potential for "full employability," if not "full employment." These thinkers prioritize the development of human potential and a "knowledge economy," utilizing technological advancements to create a modern, globally competitive economy in which even smaller countries such as New Zealand must adapt to contribute to the capitalist stage. They are "tech savvy" and see the rapid growth of technology as critical to achieving their vision of a mobile, connected modern economy.

paraphrase Labour’s 1999 Manifesto put it thus: 
Labour’s vision for the 21st century for New Zealand is of a knowledge-based, high skills, high income, high employment economy underpinned by an active government committed to increasing equality and building strong social services. To achieve this will mean stimulating world-class innovation and skills development capable of sustaining New Zealand as a leading knowledge-based economy.

According to Labour's 1999 Manifesto, their vision for New Zealand in the 21st century is to have a high-income and high-employment economy that is based on knowledge and skills, with an active government committed to equality and strong social services. To achieve this vision, it is necessary to encourage world-class innovation and skills development capable of sustaining New Zealand as a leading knowledge-based economy.
paraphrase The NZC policy promotes ICT learning where students learn to be innovative developers of products and systems and discerning consumers who will make a difference in the world.

The New Zealand Curriculum (NZC) policy encourages the teaching of information and communication technology (ICT), with an emphasis on developing student skills in innovation and product/system development. Additionally, students are taught to be discerning consumers who have the ability to make a positive impact on the world.
paraphrase Since its inception, human capital theory has been subject to repeated and often devastating critiques.
Human capital theory has faced numerous critiques since its creation, many of which have been severe and damaging.
Social Efficiency theory

Social Efficiency theory is an ideology of education that emphasizes the practical application of education to prepare individuals for success in the workplace and society. The theory posits that education should focus on preparing students with skills and knowledge that can be utilized in the economy, with an emphasis on efficiency and productivity. Social Efficiency theory also tends to prioritize standardized testing and measurable outcomes to assess student progress and success.

paraphrase Social Efficiency educators believe curriculum objectives must be stated in behavioural terms: as observable skills, as capabilities for action, as activities people can perform, as demonstrable things people can do (Schiro, 2013). The need to state objectives this way provides several insights into the Social Efficiency ideology. First, the behavioural phrasing of objectives reflects the Social Efficiency conception of the nature of man: the essence of man is expressed in the specific behaviours he can perform. Important here are both the behavioural conception of man and the conception of man as a bundle of specific skills. As Bobbitt wrote, “human life, however varied, consists in the performance of specific activities”.  Second, the behavioural phrasing of objectives reflects the Social Efficiency conception of the nature of education. As Tyler (1949) wrote, “education is a process of changing the behaviour of people. Educational objectives, then, represent the kinds of changes in behaviour that an educational institution seeks to bring about in its students”. The essence of education can be summed up by the phrase “changing behavior.” Third, the behavioural phrasing of curriculum objectives allows them to be stated in a form which facilitates the efficient scientific development of curriculum: a form that “provides clear specifications to indicate just what the educational job is”, a form that is “most helpful in selecting learning experiences and in guiding teaching”, and a form that makes evaluation easy.

Social Efficiency educators believe that curriculum objectives should be phrased in terms of observable behaviors, skills, and activities that demonstrate the capabilities of individuals. This approach provides several insights into the Social Efficiency ideology. Firstly, phrasing objectives in this way reflects the belief that the essence of individuals can be expressed through the specific behaviors they exhibit, as well as the specific skills they possess. This concept considers human life to consist of the performance of particular activities, as stated by Bobbitt. Secondly, the phrasing of objectives reflects the Social Efficiency view of education as a process of changing behavior. Educational institutions seek to bring about behavioral changes in their students through the educational process. Lastly, phrasing curriculum objectives in behavioral terms facilitates efficient, scientific development of curriculum. This phrasing allows for clear specifications, making it easier to select learning experiences, guide teaching, and evaluate student progress.

paraphrase Put into practice, the multitude of specific aims would be expected to create the socially efficient individual, a person exhibiting vocational efficiency, physical efficiency, efficiency in his civic responsibility and social relations and in the kind of personal culture appropriate to him. An education antithetical to that of the social efficiency advocates, an education that would expand a person's horizons and provide him with the tools to interpret and to alter his world. It is nothing less than that socializing of mind which is actively concerned in making experiences more communicable, in breaking down the barriers of social stratification which make individuals impervious to the interests of others.

Implemented in practice, the various specific objectives outlined by Social Efficiency theory are intended to create a well-rounded individual who is efficient in their vocation, physical fitness, civic responsibility, social relations, and personal culture. In contrast, an education that opposes the principles of Social Efficiency aims to broaden an individual's understanding of the world and provide them with the capacity to interpret and impact the world around them. This constitutes a Socializing of the mind, which focuses on making experiences more accessible and breaking down societal barriers that create emotional distance between individuals and their peers.

paraphrase Critical theories represent a range of theoretical perspectives and practices through which people may pursue social justice, address inequality, and work to produce a fairer, more inclusive and equitable society. Critical theorists argue that certain groups in society – because of their gender, ethnicity, ability, economic and social circumstances – have better access to opportunities that lead to success than others, and that this is unfair. They stress that inequities are systemic, based in institutions (the legal system, the economic system, religious and class institutions for instance) and that they support certain ways of thinking and behaving that privilege some people over others. To create a fairer and more just society, critical theorists ask for the critique of social structures and practices – big and small – that privilege certain groups and marginalise others.

Critical theories encompass various theoretical perspectives and practices that advocate for social justice, equality, inclusivity, and fairness in society. The proponents of critical theory argue that certain groups in society are afforded better opportunities and advantages based on their gender, ethnicity, economic and social status, and abilities, which is unfair. They argue that these inequalities are systemic and deeply rooted in institutions such as the legal and economic systems, religious institutions, and social class structures. These institutions support certain ways of thinking and behaving that privilege certain individuals and disadvantage others. To achieve a more just and equitable society, critical theorists call for critical analysis of social structures and practices that promote inequality and marginalize certain groups.

paraphrase a  socially-critical  orientation  to  the  revision  of  the  New Zealand curriculum were introduced  and were  explicit  about  grounding critical  thinking and action into pedagogical  practices (Gillespie & Culpan, 2000). In terms of policy directing practice, the explicit aim was for physical education teachers to adopt a critical pedagogy as a way of redressing the oppressive effects of scientisation, individualism and functionalism that were seen  as  woven into  previous  curriculum  documents  (Oven, 2010). This intent can also be seen in statements such as, 
Students will develop skills for identifying and critiquing the contributions  that  science,  technology  and  the  environment make   to   sporting   performance   (Ministry   of   Education,1999, p. 42) Students  are  encouraged  to  question  their  own  decisions critically  and  work  towards  improving  practices  relating  to physical  activity  within  the  school  and  wider  community.(Ministry of Education, 1999, p. 42)

In the context of revising the New Zealand curriculum, a social-critical approach was introduced, with a focus on incorporating critical thinking and action into pedagogical practices. This approach sought to address the oppressive effects of scientisation, individualism, and functionalism that were present in previous curriculum documents. Policy aimed to encourage physical education teachers to adopt a critical pedagogy to counter these effects. This intention is reflected in statements such as the need for students to develop skills to critically examine contributions from science, technology, and the environment to sporting performance. Additionally, students are urged to question their own decisions critically and work towards improving practices related to physical activity, both within the school and the wider community.
